name: Eastern Saudi (Sharqiyya) Arabic

ancestors.notes: The Eastern Province (al-Sharqiyya) of Saudi Arabia — al-Hasa/al-Ahsāʾ and al-Qaṭīf — is dialectologically part of the Gulf (khalījī) area, not the Najdi or Hejazi ones.

notes: Eastern Saudi / Sharqiyya Arabic (شرقاوي), the Arabic of the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia: al-Hasa (al-Ahsāʾ, Hofuf) and al-Qaṭīf. It is a GULF (khalījī) dialect, patterning with Bahrain and the lower Gulf rather than with Najd or the Hejaz, so it parents to ar-x-gulf and inherits the Gulf reflexes: qaf ق → [ɡ] (with /q/ robustly retained in a large stratum of local and learned lexemes — the /q/~[ɡ] alternation is the central topic of Al-Taisan 2022 on Hasawi; Smeaton 1973; Prochazka 1988), kaf ك → [tʃ] affrication (allophone_rules GULF_K_AFFRICATION, inherited; Al-Taisan 2022; Aljumah 2008), interdentals ث /θ/ ذ /ð/ retained, and the Gulf /ɡ/ → [dʒ] affrication of the qaf reflex. DEFINING DELTA modeled here: jīm ج → [j] (yōd) as the primary reflex — the Baḥārna-type lenition of *ǧīm to a glide, characteristic of the sedentary Shiʿi communities that dominate al-Qaṭīf and are strongly present in al-Hasa (Johnstone 1967; Holes 2004 on Bahrani Arabic; Al-Taisan 2022 lists /dʒ/→[j] lenition as a shared Northern/Eastern Arabian feature). The affricate [dʒ] is kept as the secondary variant, the reflex of the Sunni/ʿujmān (Bedouin-descended) speakers of the same region — the Eastern Province is sect- and origin-split on this segment, so [j] is modeled as the majority-Qaṭīfi reflex, not a universal. NO ar-SA umbrella node: this leaf demonstrates why — Eastern Saudi is Gulf, southwestern Saudi is peninsular-archaic, Najd is Najdi and the Hejaz is Hejazi; there is no pan-Saudi phonology, so ar-SA stays unassigned (Prochazka 1988 surveys the country precisely as a patchwork). INPUT CONTRACT: transcribes orthography+harakat; short vowels surface only when the input is vocalised.

sources.author: Al-Taisan, H.
sources.title: Velar, uvular and pharyngeal alternations in Hasawi Arabic: a Harmonic Serialism OT account (PhD thesis)
sources.notes: Open-access. On Hasawi Arabic (al-Hasa, Eastern Province). Consonant inventory has both /q/ and /ɡ/ phonemic and the affricate /tʃ/; qaf → [ɡ] with /q/ retained in a large lexical stratum (Smeaton 1973; Prochazka 1988; Mstafawi 2006). Lists the affrication of /k/→[tʃ] and the lenition of /dʒ/→[j] (Johnstone 1967) as characteristic Eastern/Northern Arabian features. The thesis's core is the /q/~/ʁ/ uvular alternation, which is lexically conditioned and left unmodelled here.

sources.author: Holes, C.
sources.title: Modern Arabic: Structures, Functions, and Varieties
sources.notes: Bahrani (Baḥārna) Arabic — the sedentary Shiʿi Gulf type of al-Qaṭīf and Bahrain — has [j]/[y] for *ǧīm and [ɡ] for *qāf; the ʿArab/Sunni type keeps the affricate. Basis for modeling ج → [j] as the majority Eastern Saudi reflex with [dʒ] as the variant.

sources.author: Johnstone, T.M.
sources.title: Eastern Arabian Dialect Studies
sources.notes: The foundational survey of the Gulf/Eastern Arabian dialects, including the *ǧīm → [j] lenition and *k → [tʃ] / *q-reflex → [dʒ] affrication that define the area.

sources.author: Prochazka, T.
sources.title: Saudi Arabian Dialects
sources.publisher: Kegan Paul International (Library of Arabic Linguistics, Monograph 8)
sources.notes: The first country-wide survey of Saudi dialects, north to south. Documents the Eastern Province (Hofuf/al-Hasa) as a Gulf-type area with qaf → [ɡ], and the country as a dialect patchwork with no single 'Saudi' phonology — grounds the decision to leave ar-SA unassigned.

location.notes: Al-Hofuf, al-Hasa, Eastern Province, Saudi Arabia.

stress.notes: Quantity-sensitive Arabic stress, inherited from the Gulf parent; see ar-SA-x-najd for the cascade and input contract.
